steps.title: Interpret the prediction criterion
steps.content: The question asks: for how many points does the line predict a greater $y$-value than actually occurs? This means predicted $y$ > actual $y$. Visually on the scatter plot, these points fall below the line of best fit.

steps.title: Establish the line's equation
steps.content: The line passes through approximately $(5, 5)$ with slope $\approx 0.8$. This gives us $y = 0.8x + 1$. We can use this to calculate the predicted $y$-value at each data point's $x$-coordinate.

steps.title: Count all points below the line
steps.content:
Check all 11 data points against their predicted values:
  • $(1, 3)$: predicted $1.8$ < actual $3$ → above
  • $(2, 3)$: predicted $2.6$ < actual $3$ → above
  • $(3, 2.5)$: predicted $3.4$ > actual $2.5$ → below ✓
  • $(4, 3)$: predicted $4.2$ > actual $3$ → below ✓
  • $(4.5, 4)$: predicted $4.6$ > actual $4$ → below ✓
  • $(5, 5)$: predicted $5$ = actual $5$ → on line
  • $(6, 7)$: predicted $5.8$ < actual $7$ → above
  • $(7, 7)$: predicted $6.6$ < actual $7$ → above
  • $(8, 7)$: predicted $7.4$ > actual $7$ → below ✓
  • $(9, 8)$: predicted $8.2$ > actual $8$ → below ✓
  • $(10, 7.5)$: predicted $9$ > actual $7.5$ → below ✓
Counting the points with checkmarks: 6 points have predicted $y$-values greater than actual values.
